text: A laparoscopic bilateral transabdominal pre-parentonial inguinal hernia repair with a single mesh. We present in this video the case of a fifty six year old male patient who presented with bilateral painful inguinal hernias. The patient past medical history showed no previous intervention. We decided to perform a laparoscopic bilateral transabdominal preperitoneal repair with a single mesh, the so-called bat mesh.
A bat-shaped polypropylene mesh measuring 29 by 12 centimeters is displayed, cut from a rectangular monofilament standard weight polypropylene prosthesis.
The easiest way to shape the mesh is to fold it in a half before starting to cut it out.

The surgeon uses a 12mm peri-umbilical optical trocar and two 5mm trocars to explore the abdomen and both inguinal fossae. A direct and an indirect hernia are identified on the right side, while a direct hernia with adhesions between the sigmoid colon and abdominal wall is found on the left side. The peritoneum is opened on the right side, and gentle dissection of the two right inguinal hernias begins.

The surgeon carefully exposes Cooper's ligament and continues dissection towards the contralateral side in the retropubic space. The right direct hernia defect becomes clearly visible, while the indirect hernia has already been reduced.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection to remove adhesions on the left side, then opens the peritoneum to expose and dissect the left direct hernia, revealing the hernia defect.
The surgeon performs medial dissection in the retropubic space, creating a tunnel that connects to the previously dissected contralateral side.

The surgeon introduces a rolled-up mesh prosthesis through the twelve millimeter optical trocar. Using laparoscopic forceps, the mesh is maneuvered from the left side through the retropubic tunnel. A reabsorbable stay suture is pulled to pass the prosthesis from the left to the right side. The mesh is then deployed and oriented, with reabsorbable stitches of varying lengths used as landmarks on the shaped prosthesis.
The surgeon secures the batmesh prosthesis over the hernia defects using tacks placed on both sides and at the center of the mesh, ensuring complete coverage of the defects.

The surgeon centers the VAT mesh prosthesis on the median line without using glue, relying on the retropubic portion to prevent dislocation. The peritoneum is then closed with a running barbed reabsorbable suture.

The operative time was one hundred twenty minutes. The postoperative hospital stay was regular and the patient was dismissed on postoperative day one. Four months after the operation the patient was completely asymptomatic.
